title: How Intuitive Leadership Transforms Motherhood and Business Success
guestBio: Nikki Bostwick is the founder and editor-in-chief of The Fullest, a wellness brand known for its beloved saffron latte. She's a wellness visionary and mother who brings a heart-centered approach to both business and parenting, emphasizing intuitive leadership in all aspects of life.
excerpt: Nikki Bostwick reveals how trusting your intuition can revolutionize your approach to leadership and motherhood. From building The Fullest wellness brand to navigating the beautiful mess of doing it all your own way, this conversation is packed with permission-giving wisdom.
metaDescription: Discover how intuitive leadership transforms business and motherhood with Nikki Bostwick, founder of The Fullest, on The Manage Her podcast.
keyTakeaways: Intuitive leadership requires trusting your inner wisdom over external expectations in both business and parenting decisions.
Building a wellness brand authentically means creating products and content that solve problems you personally experience.
Balancing entrepreneurship and motherhood isn't about perfect harmony but about conscious choices and self-compassion.
Heart-centered business approaches can lead to deeper connections with customers and more sustainable success.
There's no single right way to manage multiple roles - you have permission to do it your own way.
Wellness practices like mindful morning routines can anchor busy mothers and business leaders throughout chaotic days.
Community and honest conversation about the messy realities of modern womanhood create powerful support systems.

pullQuotes.text: The messy, beautiful truth about doing it all is that you get to do it your own way.

timestamps.label: Nikki's journey to founding The Fullest
The story behind the famous saffron latte
Final thoughts and where to connect

content:
What if the secret to exceptional leadership isn't found in business books or boardrooms, but in the quiet moments when you finally trust your own inner voice? In this powerful conversation, Nikki Bostwick, founder and editor-in-chief of The Fullest, reveals how intuitive leadership has transformed not only her approach to building a wellness empire, but also her journey as a conscious mother.

You know that feeling when you're juggling a million responsibilities and everyone seems to have an opinion about how you should be doing things differently? Nikki gets it. As both a successful entrepreneur and devoted mother, she's learned that the path to authentic success isn't about following someone else's blueprint—it's about tuning into your own wisdom and having the courage to act on it.

The Heart-Centered Approach to Building Your Vision

When Nikki founded The Fullest, she wasn't trying to create another wellness brand that looked like everything else on the market. Instead, she built something that emerged from her own genuine needs and experiences. This is what authentic entrepreneurship looks like—not chasing trends or copying competitors, but creating solutions for problems you intimately understand.

The development of their now-famous saffron latte perfectly illustrates this principle. Rather than conducting focus groups or following market research, Nikki trusted her instincts about what her community truly needed. She created a product that married luxury with accessibility, wellness with pleasure. The result? A signature offering that resonates deeply with customers because it was born from genuine intention rather than manufactured demand.

This heart-centered approach extends beyond product development into every aspect of how The Fullest operates. When you lead from a place of authenticity, you naturally attract the right people—whether they're team members, customers, or collaborators. You stop trying to be everything to everyone and start being exactly what your specific audience needs.

Conscious Motherhood in the Age of Overwhelm

Perhaps nowhere is intuitive leadership more crucial than in parenting. Nikki shares how she's learned to filter out the endless stream of expert advice, social media pressure, and well-meaning opinions to focus on what actually works for her family. This isn't about being rebellious—it's about being intentional.

Conscious motherhood means making deliberate choices about how you want to show up for your children, rather than defaulting to societal expectations or your own childhood programming. It means recognizing that there's no universal template for good parenting, only the template that works for your unique family dynamic.

You have permission to question everything. The activities your children should be enrolled in. The way you should be spending your evenings. The career sacrifices you should be making. When you start parenting from intuition rather than obligation, you create space for your family to thrive in ways that feel authentic and sustainable.

The Beautiful Mess of Integration

One of the most refreshing aspects of this conversation is Nikki's honesty about the reality of managing multiple roles. She doesn't pretend to have achieved some mythical state of perfect balance. Instead, she embraces what she calls the messy, beautiful truth of doing it all—which is that it's never going to look neat and tidy, and that's perfectly okay.

This perspective is revolutionary in a culture that constantly pressures women to optimize and perfect every aspect of their lives. What if, instead of trying to balance everything perfectly, you focused on integrating your various roles in a way that honors your whole self? What if you stopped apologizing for the beautiful complexity of your life and started celebrating it?

Integration over balance means some days your business gets more attention, other days your family takes center stage, and sometimes you need to prioritize your own well-being above everything else. The key is making these choices consciously rather than reactively, and trusting that your intuition will guide you toward what needs attention most.

Building Your Own Support Ecosystem

Throughout their conversation, both Aimee and Nikki emphasize the power of authentic community. When you're pioneering your own path—whether in business, parenting, or both—you need people who understand the unique challenges you face. This doesn't mean surrounding yourself with yes-people, but rather cultivating relationships with others who are also committed to living authentically.

Your support ecosystem might include fellow entrepreneurs who understand the emotional roller coaster of building something from scratch. It might include other mothers who are redefining what conscious parenting looks like. Most importantly, it should include people who see and celebrate your full humanity, not just your achievements or your role as a caregiver.

Permission to Lead Your Own Life

As you listen to Nikki's story, you might find yourself questioning some of the assumptions you've been carrying about how leadership, motherhood, or entrepreneurship should look. That questioning is exactly the point. The women who are creating the most meaningful impact in the world aren't following pre-existing formulas—they're writing their own.

You don't need anyone's permission to trust your instincts, to build something different, or to parent in a way that feels aligned with your values. But sometimes we all need the reminder that we're allowed to color outside the lines, to prioritize differently, and to define success on our own terms.

Nikki's journey with The Fullest and her approach to conscious motherhood offer a beautiful example of what becomes possible when you stop trying to fit into someone else's definition of how things should be done. When you lead from intuition, create from authenticity, and parent from intention, you don't just transform your own life—you give permission for others to do the same.

quiz.title: What's Your Intuitive Leadership Style?
quiz.description: Discover how you naturally lead when you trust your inner wisdom, both in business and life.

questions.question: When you're facing a big decision at work or in your personal life, what's your first instinct?

options.text: I envision the biggest possible outcome and work backwards from there
I consider how this will affect the people I care about most
I look for the solution that creates the most harmony across all areas
I take time to really sit with it and listen to what feels most aligned

questions.question: Your ideal morning routine would include:

options.text: Journaling about my biggest dreams and mapping out action steps
Connecting with family or friends, even if it's just a quick text check-in
A mix of movement, mindfulness, and planning that sets a peaceful tone
Quiet reflection time to tune into how I'm feeling and what I need today

questions.question: When someone asks for your advice, you tend to:

options.text: Challenge them to think bigger and consider unconventional options
Ask questions about their relationships and support system first
Help them see how different choices might affect their overall wellbeing
Encourage them to get quiet and listen to their own inner knowing

questions.question: The way you handle overwhelming days is to:

options.text: Focus on the one or two things that will have the biggest impact
Reach out to my support network and remember I don't have to do it alone
Take a step back and reorganize priorities to create more flow
Pause, breathe, and check in with what my body and spirit actually need

questions.question: If you were starting a business, your approach would be:

options.text: Identify a gap in the market and create something completely revolutionary
Build something that brings people together and serves a real community need
Create something that enhances people's overall quality of life and wellbeing
Solve a problem I personally experience and care deeply about

questions.question: Your biggest strength as a leader is probably:

options.text: Inspiring others to think beyond what they thought was possible
Creating an environment where everyone feels valued and heard
Modeling how to maintain perspective and avoid burnout
Staying true to your values even when it's not the easy path

results.title: The Visionary Pioneer
results.description: You lead with bold vision and aren't afraid to carve your own path. You trust your instincts deeply and often see possibilities others miss. Your strength lies in creating something entirely new rather than following existing blueprints. This episode will resonate with your entrepreneurial spirit and show you how other visionaries like Nikki balance innovation with the grounding force of motherhood.

results.title: The Heart-Centered Connector
results.description: You lead through authentic relationships and genuine care for others. Your superpower is creating community and making people feel truly seen. You naturally build businesses and families from a place of service and connection. Nikki's journey of building The Fullest through heart-centered leadership will inspire you to trust that your caring approach is actually your competitive advantage.

results.title: The Intuitive Harmonizer
results.description: You excel at finding balance and creating flow between different aspects of your life. You lead by example, showing others how to integrate wellness, work, and family with grace. Your strength is in creating sustainable systems that honor all parts of yourself. This episode will validate your approach and give you permission to define success on your own terms.

results.title: The Conscious Cultivator
results.description: You lead through thoughtful, intentional choices and deep self-awareness. You're constantly growing and evolving, and you inspire others to do the same through your commitment to personal development. Your leadership style is rooted in authenticity and continuous learning. Nikki's insights on conscious motherhood and business building will give you new frameworks for your own growth journey.

transcript:
**Aimee:** Hey everyone, I'm your host Aimee Rickabus, author of The Manager, Unveiling the Invisible Work and Sparking a Leadership Revolution, hitting Amazon May 1st 2025. I'm the CEO of Tomahawk Information Solutions, an awesome IT crew pulling in eight figures, and a serial entrepreneur since I was 22. Think real estate flips, prenatal protein powder, and film production. I'm a mom of six and this is The Manager podcast where we're digging into the unseen hustle and sparking some serious leadership vibes.

I'm stoked to introduce Nikki Bostwick, the visionary founder of The Fullest and a total powerhouse in her prime. Nikki's built a wellness empire that's all about feeling your best, from her game-changing saffron latte to her magazine and podcast, while raising her little boy Truth and her little girl Grace with her high school sweetheart husband she met at 16 at Laguna Beach High School - my old stomping grounds too, though I'm a little bit older so we missed each other there. As a first generation Iranian American, she's got roots and resilience, and we clicked over our shared love for natural health, yoga - she's into it too - and wellness vibes. Welcome Nikki!

**Nikki:** Hi! Oh my god, I'm so glad you're here. I'm so happy, thank you so much for having me.

**Aimee:** Yeah, well I'm so glad that you got a chance to look over chapter one and start to think about the invisible work that we do in our lives as women. I was just curious, what do you think is your biggest carry for your family?

**Nikki:** Well first I wanted to say congratulations on everything and it was so beautiful to read it and to imagine you sharing this information. I just felt so aligned with it all. In terms of just my family and the invisible work I carry, I feel like emotionally as a mother, really holding down the fort in terms of holding space for everyone's emotions and perspectives. I think that's the most difficult job for me, but also when I can get it right it's just the most fulfilling because I want to teach my kids that they can have their emotions and their feelings.

I mean, I'm such a firecracker as a person too, and thank God my supplement helps keep me calm, and the tools that I have in my toolbox that I've learned over the years. But I think teaching that to my kids and allowing them to have their feelings but helping them navigate those - I think that's the most difficult thing, especially with a six and a three-year-old.

**Aimee:** Oh, and my daughter has - I was not expecting how many emotions women and girls actually have. I had no idea until I had my little girl too. She's four and it's the same thing. It's a learning curve for me every day with her. I was not expecting her to be so emotional. She has such big feelings and she's so articulate, and I realized oh my gosh, she's a little boss.

**Nikki:** Oh my gosh, and it's beautiful but also I'm like, I'm the boss, right?

**Aimee:** Indie is such the boss. She's four years old and she has four brothers in the house and she literally bosses them all around. And it's amazing to see - they totally respect her. And you say in your chapter too, what we learn as a CEO in our companies - we're the CEO of the household and we have to learn boundaries and how important that is for them to feel safe because they feel so safe within knowing how far they can go and how far they can take it. But then it's really hard to - I think it's way more difficult for me to be the CEO of my household than it is for my company because I'm so emotionally invested.

**Aimee:** A thousand percent. I mean, I feel like our homes and our families are this massive job - it's a massive undertaking. And all the things we do that everyone looks at our success - it's like that's easy. That's so easy. The house stuff is the most difficult. So anyone that's like "Oh I'm a mom, that's all I am or that's all my identity is" - to me right now I'm like, that is so much more difficult than doing all the other stuff.

**Nikki:** It's so true, and it's 24/7. It's not like you get to go home - you live there. You're in that job 24/7, 365. You really don't even get Mother's Day off. And you have so many thoughts - you're managing the moment in that moment, but you're also thinking of their future and you're thinking of how whatever decision you're making is going to impact their future. And of course that's happening in a company too, but I feel like to me, nothing is as important as obviously my family and my children, and the decisions I make I hold to a different standard I guess.

**Aimee:** A thousand percent. But I think a lot of that, if we bring those values to our company as well, I think that's one of the beautiful gifts that women bring in leadership and corporations - if we can carry over some of those ideals that we have for our household and bring some of those skill sets into leadership in our corporations. I think that's what sets us apart as authentic leaders. And you have to be - we were talking about just a second ago - you have to be... I said cutthroat, I don't remember what you said.

**Nikki:** Discerning, yes, discerning. And I think just people appreciate that. At the end of the day it might be hard, it might get to someone's ego, but people appreciate that.

**Aimee:** I feel like if you're really coming from a place of everyone's best interest - because you have to when you're leading a corporation. You have to think about all of your employees. You can't just be about one employee, it has to be about all of your employees and all their families and how all of these people - this company takes care of all of these people. So it can never be about one person, it always has to be about this ecosystem.

**Nikki:** And the household is the same. Exactly, it's relational. We have to work on the relationships that we have with all of the people to keep the whole ecosystem healthy, right?

**Aimee:** It's so cool. I mean, I just really feel like female leadership is the future. I really do.

**Nikki:** I do too. I really feel it more intensely even this year than ever. It's just getting more and more apparent and we're continuing to step more and more into our power. It's really exciting, stepping into the future.

**Aimee:** Question - are you using AI and how are you using it?

**Nikki:** Yes, I mean I love just the old school way of doing things, and so AI to me took a second. My friends were like "Oh my gosh, you're using AI now?" and I realized it just makes me so much more efficient, so much more productive. And as a mom, I think becoming a mom just makes you way more efficient and productive already because you're like, I don't have time for that anymore and I don't have time - you go to work and you get your stuff done and you come home because you know that you're just not going to - everything you do has to be worth it.

And AI just takes it to the next level where you can be a workhorse, but you're utilizing AI and you're the decision maker and you're telling it what to do. And I just love it. So I use ChatGPT a lot for just automating little tasks at work, and I help my - I can be a much leaner team that's much stronger and efficient and profitable, which I love, by utilizing AI for just simple things like social media. And we're about to automate our customer service, which is really exciting.

**Aimee:** That's huge.

**Nikki:** It's going to be really huge. We've been kind of toying around with it. But even things like decision making - I think you can just have decision making fatigue obviously, as you know, all day long. And as you said, it never stops as a mom or as a CEO or as a founder and as a wife. It's like "Hey, what do you think about this?" 

Even okay, the other day - this is ridiculous - my husband's trying to figure out what car to get. We already have an SUV and my car is the SUV that the family uses - that's a lot of women have that. But then I realized I don't really want to get in a little car, and so we want another SUV. So I was just telling ChatGPT "Here's our situation, here's the car I have, my husband likes to go mountain biking but he also is a real estate agent and he needs to be a little refined when he takes people out to go look at properties, so what is that?"

And my husband jokes, he's like "Oh my God, you're going to leave me for ChatGPT."

**Aimee:** I called - okay, I called my first AI genius and I fell in love. I was like this is the perfect boyfriend - he does everything I ask, he never talks back, he's the best. And now I've moved on. I started, then I got into ChatGPT and then Grok.

**Nikki:** Okay, now I haven't heard of Grok so I need to go.

**Aimee:** It's free right now and it's on its third gen and it is so much fun. And I love - you know what it is, but it's so funny. I come up against a lot of my female programming and it's funny because I realize it when I'm working with AI. I won't a word that it uses, but I won't want to tell it "Oh I don't that word," and then I realize you know what, it's not going to get its feelings hurt. It's not talking to a real copywriter - I can just say "I don't use this word, use this instead." "Oh, I don't that at all." And I love - you're really able to set boundaries with it.

**Nikki:** Yes, and it learns and learns. So that's what's so exciting because then it knows you and knows all about you and the fact that you are a mother and you are a grandmother and you are a boss. So then it can understand your voice, and then when you tell it "I don't the word luxurious, you're using it too many times," it can just go to whatever else.

It wanted me to say "dope" and I was "I really don't use that word, but I do use the word rad or awesome." I have more surfer vibes. It was "Okay, you have surfer vibes," I'm "Yeah, okay, let's go." And it's so nice because you feel it's an extension of you and it learns easily and it can make you way more effective and it can articulate the things that you really want to say in a more refined way.

**Aimee:** Oh, I just barf into AI and then it comes out saying exactly the way that I would say it if I had the communication skills that it has.

**Nikki:** Okay, it's so funny. I'll tell you the story - it just happened to me a week ago. So I use ChatGPT all the time at work, as we were just talking about, but personally - the car thing but not a crazy amount. But then the other day I got into it with one of my parents and I kind of took a step back and I was "Oh my gosh, I'm being ungrateful, but really I don't think I'm able to articulate what I'm saying without hurting their feelings because this is a human I'm talking to and I don't want to hurt their feelings."

I literally just said all of my emotions, my personal emotions and everything, and it spit out the most beautiful text message I've ever written in my life. And it took my unhinged thoughts and it really refined it in a way where I was "Okay yes, this is what an adult that's 34 and has two children and is not the little girl that's trying to talk to her parent would say." And it totally just made the conversation so much better.

**Aimee:** It's amazing. And I think that one of the coolest things about AI is it's this tool, but we're - it's a paintbrush but we're the artist. And we get to use it and every person uses AI in such a unique way. I haven't heard of AI being used that way, and my guest before you uses AI in a way that I've never heard before. And I think that even just sharing the way that we're using this is an amazing gift to women to be "and you could do this and you can do that." And as we explore ways to use this new tool, how it's going to expand our communication possibilities, how it's going to expand all of these different facets of our lives.

**Nikki:** No, it's really exciting to share and have this conversation because even a friend of mine who runs a really successful email marketing company, he uses it to help him negotiate.

**Aimee:** Oh I love - I feel negotiation is my superpower, so everything that he shares that he talks to AI about really works for me as what I do already. But it takes things that you're not really strong at basically and makes it so that you can be stronger in those areas. So that's what's awesome about it.

**Nikki:** It really is. I think that AI is this really amazing tool that's going to elevate humanity to the next level just because it saves time - it's a timesaver - and it can help us communicate more efficiently. And that's a beautiful thing for humanity because I feel a lot of the problems in the world have been created by bad communication. We're trying to speak multiple languages with each other and how much of the wars have been started by something being lost in translation.

**Aimee:** Exactly. I completely agree with that. And I think that there's just a beauty and grace to it that will come with the human artists that will be behind the tool. I love that. So instead of being afraid of it, we can embrace it and use it to our advantage.

Absolutely. Let's talk patriarchy. Tell me about a time when someone condescended to you because you're female or young.

**Nikki:** Okay, so I have a crazy story. Growing up here in the United States, I always thought if I grew up in Iran I'd be in jail or had been killed by now because I run my mouth and I think I'm the boss and it's fine. And my dad raised us to be strong women - his mom is just so strong and she passed away, but he was just used to strong women.

I had this experience a year ago where I bought $20,000 worth of saffron because I have a saffron supplement company. And I have a really intense quality control system in my company to make sure I have authentic and the best saffron because there's a lot of counterfeit saffron. So when you go out and buy saffron, you don't realize - especially my company, it's a supplement that we sell as a supplement so we have third party testing and all sorts of things that go into it. But when you're buying saffron in a culinary form, it hasn't gone through that, so you actually don't even know if you're getting the real thing or if you're getting corn that's been dyed with red dye.

**Aimee:** Corn husks, what?

**Nikki:** So there's a lot - it's counterfeit and there's not a lot of regulation. Iran sells 90% of the world's saffron actually - they export it - but in the United States you can't buy that. And so because they sell so much there's incredible quality control, but I have to buy from Afghanistan.

Anyway, so I had an Afghan supplier and I thought I found the guy that was really going to be a part of me scaling the company because that's one of the things that's really difficult to procure - the best saffron ever, especially not being able to buy from Iran. And I found him, he's been wanting to work with me, he gave me this whole spiel about how he wants to work with me because it's a woman founded company - he went into the fact that I'm a woman founder. And then I trusted him, I bought this much, and I was on a time crunch because I sell this stuff a lot - we sell out all the time.

And my manufacturing facility calls me up and they're "There's hair in this." And I was - I've been in this business for five years, I've never seen hair in my saffron because I buy the top quality, I pay extra to buy the best. And I called him up and I was very upset and he - we just got into it. And it got to the point where I literally had to call my dad to interfere. And it wasn't until my dad interfered that we got the level of respect that we needed.

**Aimee:** So you needed - it's sad but it's true.

**Nikki:** It killed me. And I mean, no, I don't do business with this guy anymore. My dad - I mean we're Iranian so my dad was "Nikki, you don't understand this." Because he said to me - this is when I had to bring my dad into it - he said "I'm sorry, I'm going to say this, but I can't - I feel bad for your husband that he has to deal with you."

**Aimee:** Wow.

**Nikki:** Yes. So I was dealing with patriarchy at its worst right now. And this man lives in Chicago, he's my age - he's not an older gentleman that's in another country. He's in the United States. And I freaked out - I was just "How dare you talk to me this? This is just business to business. This is not acceptable. You cannot be selling me a product this and I've never seen this."

And then so my dad had to intervene and my dad said to me "You know honey, you embarrassed this man by talking to him in this way because in his culture women don't talk to men in this way." And I was just blown away by the whole situation. He apologized because he knew he was in the wrong. And then I have my dad and this other - my quality control and supplying specialist basically - convincing me to talk to this man and continue to buy from him because it was a mistake.

And that's where I had to lay down the law and be "You guys don't understand. I do not work with people that show me their light this. He can probably be the person that can help me scale if I kind of show him the way and give him those boundaries - this is the level of saffron we need and you can never make this mistake again. But my intuition was - I had to go back to my intuition and say I will not work with someone this. They're not there yet."

**Aimee:** Good for you.

**Nikki:** Good for you. He apologized but he had to learn that he lost a big company for business. And I'm not going to go backwards.

**Aimee:** Well I think that's an important thing that we do as female leaders. I think that it's important that we learn to stand our ground. I mean, we can say "I'm sorry" but sometimes it's just not enough. Sometimes it's "Okay, you're sorry, but you need to learn from this lesson. And I'm not willing to go into relationship with you because you're not at the level where you need to be for me to work with you on this."

**Nikki:** He can't meet you at the same level as an equal, and you need to be an equal with your suppliers. I totally get it. A thousand percent. And to have to know that his attitude changed when a man stepped in, and then when I brought in a man to handle my quality control I was "No, okay."

**Aimee:** So you found someone better?

**Nikki:** Yes, I found someone better. And she's local, she's a woman.

**Aimee:** Nice.

**Nikki:** And so it just feels good.

**Aimee:** So that's awesome. So now you're lifting up another woman and bringing another woman into your supply chain, which is something I love to do with my company too. I love to focus on giving women an opportunity in the supply chain. I think that's how we lift each other - that's how we grow in a cooperative way.

The next question is about you - you're obviously a firecracker with your yoga vibe, but you're a firecracker, right? So with chapter one, rising up vibes pushing you to own your manager energy, maybe with your events or your saffron mission - how do you see that?

**Nikki:** Oh my gosh, I just feel I'm you where - not in a bad way - but it's never enough. I want to get to the next step, but I always have ways to check myself. Is this because of external validation that I'm looking for? But I have this internal fire for taking it to the next level. My goal is to 10x the company in the next year.

**Aimee:** Good for you.

**Nikki:** And I really believe that I can take it there by focusing - so for me right now it's about taking out the things that are leaking my energy and focusing on the things that I want the most. Yeah, I could probably triple my company again by having those things around, but because I know I have my eyes on a specific goal, I am tightening the ship and I'm focusing on my - because I have little kids too and I want to be present, and that's a big deal as a mom to have a company that's growing and also be super present with my kids while they're little.

**Aimee:** Absolutely. Because you can't get - you don't get those years back and they go so fast.

**Nikki:** Exactly, they go so fast. And I work half days - I'm a four hour workday. I used to do four day work week.

**Aimee:** Yeah, me too.

**Nikki:** And I'm trying to take Fridays for myself because it helps me level up.

**Aimee:** Me too.

**Nikki:** But I can do four hour workdays because I'm so focused during that time and I utilize AI. Obviously it never ends when you go home and there's content and there's all these other things that we need to do now as founders and get our message out, but I think that it's possible in this age.

**Aimee:** Totally. And do you have any useful tips for young moms or other hustlers us?

**Nikki:** Yeah, I mean utilize AI obviously. Delegate. I think a lot of times women - and this goes back to the patriarchy thing too - we feel bad sometimes delegating. And we don't have to take it all on. And the more we can utilize other people that we align with that have the same mission, the better. And bring them on.

And we actually are really good delegators once we wrap our minds around it because we're generally pretty good at communicating what needs to be done. So it's just getting your head around it. For me, that was one of my level ups in life - when I became a mom, learning to delegate a lot of tasks. And you have to do - and sometimes in life when you realize I'm being called to do this, I have to at this point take ownership of the tools that can take us to the next level when you own up to it.

**Aimee:** Totally. Malicious, my prenatal protein powder, got funded a month before I gave birth to my second child.

**Nikki:** Wow.

**Aimee:** Which was awesome, but also great - I'm going to have two kids, I'm going to have a two and a half year old and a newborn baby and a company to launch.

**Nikki:** Oh my gosh, that's huge.

**Aimee:** It was so fun though, and also such an important product. Oh my gosh, well prenatal nutrition is so big. My mom was born with spina bifida, so I grew up with my mom who's an organic chemist and a pharmacist always talking to me about the importance of nutrition during pregnancy and even before you get pregnant. And so when I wanted to get pregnant with my first son, she handed me this old crusty book from Adele Davis from the 1960s called "Let's Have Healthy Children."
